metric_value: $71.9 billion (12.1% increase over 1994)
notes: Highest revenues in IBM history at time

metric_value: $4.2 billion (38.3% earnings increase over 1994)
notes: Best earnings in recent memory at time

metric_name: IBM Global Services 1995 revenues and backlog
metric_value: $20.1B revenue; $29B backlog; 100,000 employees (of 225,000 total IBM)
notes: $7.4B maintenance + $12.7B professional fees

metric_name: IBM Global Services professional fees growth rate
metric_value: 30% annual growth for past 4 years in customer-intimate professional services
notes: vs essentially no-growth maintenance portion

metric_value: Revenue up 4.4% but hardware declined 0.6%; services (ex-maintenance) up 26.4%
notes: Aberdeen estimates hardware decline would have been ~12% without services pull-through

metric_value: 100,000 at study; on-track for 110,000 by end 1996; 45% of IBM total employees
notes: Fastest-growing portion of company

metric_name: IBM 12 global industry solution groups functional area coverage
metric_value: 140 functional areas replicable; planned increase to 170+
notes: Groups cover Banking, Distribution, Education, Government, Healthcare, Insurance, Manufacturing, Petroleum, Process, Telecom & Media, Travel, Utilities

metric_value: Be first source for installed base's next-generation information solutions; Network Computing supplier of choice globally
notes: Secondary: create new customers in China, former Soviet Union, emerging economies

metric_value: IBM commits to fulfilling any customer request for information-service solutions including using competitor products (HP Unix, Microsoft BackOffice)
notes: Major departure from hardware-centric approach; customers insisting on best-in-class non-IBM accommodated

metric_name: IBM 'One Face to the Customer' strategy
metric_value: Unified IBM presentation replacing former competing hardware divisions (S/390, AS/400, RS/6000, PC server) fighting in front of customers
notes: Services-first recommendation; hardware discounted to capture long-term relationships

metric_value: Most complete IT product line of any company; 25% of R&D staff required annually to work with customers via Global Services
notes: Direct feedback loop between customer needs and scientific development

metric_value: Closest to truly global organization: unified industry solution groups treating global customer operations as single IT project; local presence in smaller metropolitan areas
notes: vs competitors with offices only in largest cities

metric_value: Employees hardened by 1990s layoffs; motivated to prove value; no entitlement culture remaining
Installed in almost every Global 500 datacenter; knows customers' infrastructure, staff, and business processes
notes: Lowest customer onboarding cost among competitors

metric_value: Excellent for Global Services desktop deployments but lacks ISV support for Microsoft Office 95; perception of proprietary strategy haunts IBM
notes: Business unit executives don't share IS enthusiasm for IBM-dominated solutions

metric_value: Follower in RDBMS functionality and performance vs Oracle, Sybase, Informix; IS decision makers value best-in-class
notes: Hardware performance similarly lagging across full product spectrum

metric_value: IBM sold more Notes licenses in first 6 months of ownership than Lotus had in previous 6 years; Domino Notes (internet version) extends value
notes: Confirms wisdom of $3.5B acquisition for collaborative groupware

metric_value: Replication of implementations combined with Network Computing migration will increase revenues by >$8B/year in foreseeable future
notes: IBM projection cited by Aberdeen

metric_value: Y2K may be bigger and more profitable than Network Computing opportunity; IBM ideally positioned for Y2K Global Services engagement
notes: Aberdeen believes IBM will capitalize on crash-buying starting mid-1997

metric_value: OS/2's lack of ISV support and proprietary perception will continue to limit IBM's desktop influence
Independent professional service providers will downplay IBM offerings as Global Services competes against them; ISVs will view IBM as competitor
notes: Computer Associates, Oracle, Microsoft, SAP, Netscape named as co-opetitors

metric_value: By early 2000s IBM became leader in IT services and consulting; $7B+ cost avoidance 1996-1998; services dominant revenue driver
notes: Source: IBM On Demand Business Transformation document (https://public.dhe.ibm.com/software/solutions/pdfs/IBMOnDemandBusinessTransformation.pdf); Eustochos (https://eustochos.com/ibms-transformation-in-the-1990s/).

metric_value: OS/2 discontinued; IBM ended development support 2006; Aberdeen's prediction of strategic liability proved correct
notes: Microsoft Windows dominated enterprise desktop as predicted.

metric_name: IBM Global Services spin-off actual outcome
metric_value: IBM spun off infrastructure services as Kyndryl (2021); consulting retained as IBM Consulting; Aberdeen had warned against spin-off
notes: Aberdeen's 1996 warning against spinning off Global Services was ultimately not heeded 25 years later.

metric_value: IBM's Network Computing vision prefigured cloud computing and service-oriented architecture; IBM led enterprise services market through 2000s
notes: IBM's 1996 vision was substantially correct in direction though terminology evolved.

metric_value: IBM is the largest business phoenix — rising and soaring from near disaster — we may ever have the pleasure to observe
notes: Aberdeen endorses IBM transformation as successfully executing

metric_value: Investment community may begin to evaluate IBM as services provider and award higher P/E ratio than hardware companies receive
notes: Financial implication of transformation: margins lower but returns on assets higher
